title: EPIC: T8 — Swarm + Gate Machine (Agent Orchestration)
description:
## Overview
The swarm command spawns agents on the VPS via NTM and manages them through
the work phase. The gate machine controls phase transitions through the
entire flywheel pipeline with human checkpoints.

## Bead ID Mapping (IDs assigned by br, not sequential)
- afc-1a1.3 = T8.1 GateMachine
- afc-1a1.4 = T8.2 Swarm command
- afc-1a1.1 = T8.3 Gate commands
- afc-1a1.5 = T8.4 Idle detection
- afc-1a1.2 = T8.5 Rollback

## Gate State Machine
INIT → PLAN → GATE_PLAN → BEADS → GATE_BEADS → SWARM → GATE_SWARM → REVIEW → GATE_REVIEW → DEPLOY → DONE
Each GATE_* state pauses until flywheel gate advance (or --autopilot).

## Swarm Lifecycle
1. Check gate is at SWARM phase
2. Create checkpoint SHA (git rev-parse HEAD on VPS)
3. Spawn NTM session with N agents via --robot-spawn or recipe
4. Auto-provision commit agent (separate pane) unless --no-commit
5. Send swarm-init-briefing prompt to all agents
6. Enter monitoring loop (delegates to T9)
7. Budget guard: check api_calls sum every poll cycle

## Commit Agent Safety
The commit agent runs continuously with strict constraints:
- May only git add already-tracked files
- May only git commit and git push
- Must never: rebase, merge, reset --hard, checkout ., deploy, git add untracked
These rules are injected via the swarm-init-briefing prompt and are
NON-CONFIGURABLE. This is a deliberate safety constraint.

## Idle Detection
Compare snapshots for byte-identical output (3 × 5s = 15s to declare idle)
plus 30min absolute timeout. NEVER parse Claude Code output text — compare
snapshot.output_tail_lines counts only.

## Files
- cli/gate-machine.ts (state machine)
- cli/commands/swarm.ts (swarm command)
- cli/commands/gate.ts (gate commands)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Gate machine transitions through all phases correctly
- GATE_* phases pause until explicit advance
- flywheel swarm 4 spawns 4 agents + commit agent
- Budget guard stops swarm when cost exceeds limit
- Idle detection uses snapshot comparison only
- Checkpoint SHA stored for rollback

title: T8.3: flywheel gate advance/status commands
description:
## What
Implement the gate CLI commands for manual phase control.

## Commands

### flywheel gate status
Print current gate state for the active run:
```
Run #3 "habit-tracker"
Phase: GATE_SWARM (waiting for human approval)
Previous: SWARM (completed 2m ago)
Next: REVIEW
Cost so far: $12.45
```

### flywheel gate advance
Advance from current GATE_* phase to next work phase:
```
Advancing from GATE_SWARM → REVIEW
✓ Gate passed. Review phase active.
```
- Fails if not at a GATE_* phase: "Cannot advance: currently at SWARM (not a gate phase)"
- Logs phase_event with actor: 'human'

## Acceptance Criteria
- gate status shows current phase, run info, cost
- gate advance transitions from GATE_* to next phase
- gate advance fails clearly when not at a gate phase
- Both commands fail clearly if no active run

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/gate.ts (new)
- cli/index.ts (wire up)

title: T8.5: flywheel rollback (reset VPS to checkpoint SHA)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel rollback [run-id] — resets the VPS project repo to the
checkpoint SHA recorded at swarm start.

## Why
If a swarm produces bad code, the operator needs a quick escape hatch.
The checkpoint SHA was captured at swarm start (T8.2) and stored in
flywheel_runs.checkpoint_sha.

## Flow
1. Look up checkpoint_sha from flywheel_runs (current run or specified run-id)
2. Confirm with user: "Reset to <sha>? This will discard all swarm changes. [y/N]"
3. SSH to VPS: git reset --hard <sha>
4. Log phase_event: type 'rollback', payload: { sha, run_id }
5. Print: "✓ Reset to <sha>. Swarm changes discarded."

## Safety
- Requires explicit y confirmation (no -y flag)
- Only works with SHAs from flywheel_runs (cannot specify arbitrary SHA)
- Warning if uncommitted changes exist on VPS (git status --porcelain)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Rolls back to checkpoint SHA from flywheel_runs
- Requires explicit confirmation
- Warns about uncommitted changes
- Logs rollback event to phase_events

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/swarm.ts (add rollback subcommand)

title: T8.1: GateMachine class (phase state machine + transitions)
description:
## What
Implement the GateMachine class — the state machine controlling phase transitions.

## State Diagram
INIT → PLAN → GATE_PLAN → BEADS → GATE_BEADS → SWARM → GATE_SWARM → REVIEW → GATE_REVIEW → DEPLOY → DONE

## API
- currentPhase(runId): Phase
- advance(runId): Phase — moves to next phase, emits phase_event
- isGated(runId): boolean — true when at GATE_* phase
- waitForGate(runId, signal): Promise<void> — polls 5s, blocks until gate opened
- setPhase(runId, phase): void — direct set for initialization

## Rules
- Sequential transitions only (no skipping)
- No backwards movement
- Every transition logged to phase_events table
- Illegal transition throws GateError

## Files: cli/gate-machine.ts (new)

title: T8.2: flywheel swarm command (spawn + briefing + budget guard)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel swarm <count> [--no-commit] [--budget <usd>] [--recipe <name>]

## Flow
1. Assert gate at SWARM phase
2. Checkpoint SHA (git rev-parse HEAD on VPS)
3. Spawn NTM session with agents
4. Auto-provision commit agent unless --no-commit
5. Send swarm-init-briefing to all agents
6. Budget guard: poll cost every 10s, pause at limit
7. Print status and enter monitoring loop

## Budget Guard
90% warning, 100% auto-pause via ntm.pause()

## Commit Agent
Separate pane, strict safety rules, non-configurable constraints.

## Files: cli/commands/swarm.ts (new)

title: T8.4: Idle detection (snapshot comparison algorithm)
description:
## What
Implement idle detection using NTM snapshot comparison (NOT output parsing).

## Algorithm
3 identical snapshots at 5s intervals = idle (15s total)
30min absolute timeout

## Rules
- Only compare activity fields (last_output_age_sec, current_bead, pending_mail)
- Do NOT hash timestamps or volatile fields
- NEVER parse agent terminal output text
- Returns: 'active' | 'idle' | 'timeout'

## Files: cli/commands/swarm.ts (add IdleDetector class)

title: EPIC: Monitor + Local Server
description:
# Monitor + Local Server — The Bridge

## Architecture
Browser (dashboard) ←WebSocket→ flywheel CLI server (localhost:4200) ←SSH→ VPS

The server is a Node.js HTTP + ws process that:
1. Polls the VPS via SSH on fixed intervals
2. Pushes state updates to browser clients via WebSocket
3. Accepts mutation commands from browser (3 permitted actions only)

## Polling Intervals (from pitch spec)
- Agent snapshot: every 10s (--robot-snapshot)
- Beads: every 20s (br list --json)
- VPS health: every 45s (uptime + df)
- Agent mail: every 10s (snapshot.pending_mail per agent)

## Security
WebSocket connections from non-loopback addresses are immediately terminated at the upgrade event. This is a local-only tool.

## Three Permitted Mutations (nothing else)
1. prompt_send: delivers a named prompt to agents
2. swarm_pause / swarm_resume: sends ntm pause/resume
3. gate_advance: advances the current gate

## No VPS→Dashboard Push
The VPS never initiates contact with the console. Always SSH→poll→push. This keeps the VPS side stateless with respect to the console.

title: HTTP server on localhost:4200 with WebSocket (loopback-only)
Polling loops: snapshot (10s), beads (20s), VPS health (45s)
Three permitted mutation handlers + flywheel serve + flywheel monitor
EPIC: Prompt Engine
description:
# Prompt Engine — The Playbook Runtime

## What It Does
The PromptEngine class is the runtime for prompts.yaml. It resolves variables, routes prompts to the right model/effort tier, and delivers them to agents via NTM.

## Why Separate From Commands
Multiple commands use prompt sending: flywheel prompts send, the swarm loop (auto-sending swarm-init-briefing), the review command (broadcasting fresh-review). Having a shared PromptEngine prevents duplication and ensures consistent audit logging.

## Variable Resolution Priority
1. Explicit vars passed at call time (highest priority)
2. Current flywheel run context from SQLite (flywheel_runs row)
3. VPS config (ssh.yaml remote_repo_root, etc.)

title: PromptEngine class with variable resolution and send()
EPIC: Review + Deploy Commands
description:
# Review + Deploy Commands — Final Pipeline Phases

## Review Phase
flywheel review [--passes n] broadcasts the review prompt sequence to all agents:
1. fresh-review → wait for idle → peer-review
2. Run ubs scan remotely and capture output
3. Advance gate to GATE_REVIEW

## Deploy Gate (Extra Safety)
flywheel deploy requires the user to type DEPLOY <project-name> explicitly.
No -y flag. No --force. The friction is intentional — deploy is irreversible.

## Beads Commands
These wrap br commands over SSH:
- beads generate: sends beads-generate-from-plan prompt with {plan_path} var
- beads refine: sends beads-review-refine prompt
- beads triage: runs bv -emit-script, streams output
- beads history [--at sha]: runs br history --json or bv -as-of <sha>

title: flywheel review [--passes n] + UBS scan integration
description:
## What
Implement the review phase commands.

## flywheel review [--passes 2]
1. Assert phase == GATE_SWARM
2. Advance to REVIEW phase
3. For each pass (default 2):
   a. broadcast prompt: fresh-review → wait for idle (all agents) → broadcast peer-review
   b. Wait for agents to settle (idle detection, same as swarm)
4. Run UBS scan remotely
5. Capture and display UBS output
6. Advance to GATE_REVIEW
7. Print gate message

## UBS Scan Integration
```typescript
const ubsResult = await ssh.exec(`ubs ${projectDir} --format json --report-json /tmp/ubs-report.json`);
// Stream output to terminal
// Also push to WebSocket for dashboard display
broadcast({ type: 'ubs_report', data: JSON.parse(ubsResult.stdout) });
```

## Acceptance criteria
- review fails if phase != GATE_SWARM
- Each pass waits for agent idle before sending next prompt
- UBS scan runs on VPS project directory (not local)
- Gate advances to GATE_REVIEW after all passes complete

title: flywheel deploy (DEPLOY confirmation) + beads commands
description:
## What
Deploy command and beads subcommands.

## flywheel deploy
Requires explicit typed confirmation to prevent accidental deploys:
```typescript
const answer = await rl.question(`Type DEPLOY ${projectName} to confirm: `);
if (answer !== `DEPLOY ${projectName}`) {
  console.error('Deployment cancelled.');
  process.exit(1);
}
// Run configured deploy command on VPS via SSH
await ssh.exec(deployCmd, { cwd: projectDir });
```
No -y flag. No --force. The friction is intentional.

## flywheel beads generate
Sends beads-generate-from-plan prompt with {plan_path} variable.
plan_path is resolved from: flywheel_runs.notes or default ~/projects/<name>/plan.md

## flywheel beads refine
Sends beads-review-refine prompt to all agents.

## flywheel beads triage
Runs `bv -emit-script` on VPS, streams output to terminal.
This gives the agent's recommended work order.

## flywheel beads history [--at <sha>]
Runs `br history --json` (or `bv -as-of <sha>`) on VPS.
Shows bead state at a point in time.

## Acceptance criteria
- deploy rejects anything except exact "DEPLOY <project-name>" string
- beads generate fails clearly if plan.md not found on VPS
- beads history --at <sha> passes SHA to bv -as-of correctly

title: EPIC: NTM Bridge
description:
# NTM Bridge — The Substrate Wrapper

## Why NTM Is The Substrate
NTM (Named Tmux Manager v1.2.0) is the execution layer on the VPS. It spawns agent sessions, sends prompts, monitors state, and coordinates across panes. We never bypass NTM — we wrap it.

## The Robot API
NTM exposes --robot-* flags that emit machine-readable JSON. This is our entire interface:
- --robot-snapshot: per-agent state with confidence scores, last_output_age_sec, pending_mail
- --robot-status: full session/agent/graph state with tmux pane IDs
- --robot-send: broadcast/targeted prompt dispatch with success/failure per pane
- --robot-spawn: create session with agents
- --robot-recipes: 6 named presets (quick-claude, full-stack, minimal, etc.)
- --robot-markdown: human-readable status table for display

## Critical PATH Issue
SSH non-interactive sessions strip PATH. NTM lives in ~/.local/bin. Every remote call MUST use:
  bash -lc 'ntm --robot-snapshot'
OR prefix PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH explicitly.
This is the #1 gotcha. Get it wrong and every call silently fails with "command not found".

## Pane ID Inconsistency (Known NTM Bug)
--robot-status returns pane IDs as tmux format: "%41"
--robot-snapshot returns pane IDs as "window.pane": "0.1"
The bridge layer normalizes to "window.pane" format internally. Never let raw pane IDs leak to upper layers.

## JSON Extraction Guard
NTM sometimes emits log lines before/after the JSON blob. Always extract JSON with a regex:
  raw.match(/({[\s\S]*}|[[\s\S]*])/)
Never trust that stdout IS the JSON.

## agent-mail Access
agent-mail is an MCP server — NOT shell-accessible. Mail monitoring uses snapshot.pending_mail count per agent. No direct mail querying from the console in v1.

title: TypeScript interfaces for all NTM robot API response shapes
description:
## What
Define ALL TypeScript interfaces for NTM robot API responses in cli/ntm-bridge.ts. These are derived from live VPS output captured on 2026-03-09.

## Interfaces to Define

### NtmAgent (from --robot-snapshot)
```typescript
interface NtmAgent {
  pane: string;              // "0.1" format (window.pane)
  type: 'claude' | 'codex' | 'gemini' | 'unknown';
  type_confidence: number;   // 0.0-1.0
  type_method: string;       // "content" | "unknown"
  state: 'active' | 'idle' | 'error';
  last_output_age_sec: number;  // -1 = unknown (agent just started)
  current_bead: string | null;
  pending_mail: number;
}
```

### NtmSession, NtmSnapshot, NtmStatus, NtmSendResult, NtmRecipe
(Full interfaces from live API responses)

## IMPORTANT: Two Pane ID Formats
--robot-status uses tmux format: "%41", "%40"
--robot-snapshot uses window.pane format: "0.1", "0.2"
These refer to the same panes! The bridge normalizes to window.pane format.

## Acceptance criteria
- All interfaces exported from cli/ntm-bridge.ts
- NtmAgent.last_output_age_sec = -1 is handled (not treated as 1 second ago)
- TypeScript strict mode: no `any` types in these interfaces

title: ntmRobot() base wrapper: JSON extraction + bash -lc PATH fix
Pane ID normalization: %41 ↔ 0.1 format
description:
## What
NTM has an inconsistency between its two main status endpoints:
- --robot-status returns tmux native IDs: "%41", "%40"
- --robot-snapshot returns window.pane format: "0.1", "0.2"

These refer to the same physical pane. The bridge must normalize to a single canonical format.

## Decision: Canonical Format = "window.pane" (e.g., "0.1")
This format is used by --robot-snapshot which is our most-used polling endpoint.

## Implementation
```typescript
// Called on --robot-status output before returning to callers
function normalizePaneId(raw: string): string {
  // Already normalized: "0.1", "1.2" etc.
  if (/^\d+\.\d+$/.test(raw)) return raw;
  // tmux format %41 — cannot reliably convert without querying tmux
  // Store both and let callers match by position index instead
  return raw;
}
```

## Practical Approach
Rather than trying to mathematically convert between formats (which requires knowing the window layout), the bridge should:
1. Use --robot-snapshot for all agent state (window.pane format)
2. Use --robot-status only for high-level session metadata (not pane IDs)
3. Never expose raw pane IDs to upper layers — use session+index instead

## Acceptance criteria
- NtmBridge never leaks %41-format IDs to callers
- Upper layers address agents by {session, paneIndex} not raw pane ID
- Comment in code explains the format inconsistency for future maintainers

EPIC: T11 — Vite Dashboard (React + Tailwind + TanStack Query/Form/Virtual)
description:
## Overview
The dashboard is a Vite + React + Tailwind SPA that runs at localhost:5173
and talks to the local server (T9) through a stable HTTP API plus a
read-only live event bridge at localhost:4200/ws. HTTP is the source of truth
for app state; WebSocket is only used to patch or invalidate cached data and
stream stdout lines.

## Architecture
```
Browser (5173) → Vite dev proxy → Server (4200) → SSH → VPS
```

The Vite dev server proxies /ws and /api to the backend server. The frontend
uses TanStack Query for server state, TanStack Form for prompt/settings
workflows, and TanStack Virtual for high-volume log rendering. TanStack Table
is optional for richer row-model filtering on large datasets.

## 6 Panels (from pitch)
1. PipelineView — phase pipeline visualization + gate button
2. BeadBoard — kanban board with bead stats
3. AgentPanel — per-agent status, type, mail count, current bead
4. PromptLibrary — browse + send prompts with validated variables
5. LogStream — live stdout feed (virtualized)
6. MemoryPanel — cass/cm session memory

## Key Design Decisions
- Query cache is the dashboard data spine
- WebSocket events update or invalidate query keys, not panel-local state
- Log lines stay in a dedicated ring buffer store, not in TanStack Query
- Status indicator in navbar is driven by the live bridge connection state
- Loopback-only server: no auth, no tokens, no CORS

## Files
- dashboard/ (entire directory — new Vite project)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Dashboard loads at localhost:5173
- Initial bootstrap data comes from HTTP within 10s
- Live events refresh visible panels after server-side changes
- All 6 panels render with real data
- Log stream handles 1000+ lines without jank

title: T11.2: Dashboard app shell — QueryClient, typed API client, live cache bridge
description:
## What
Build the dashboard app shell around TanStack Query instead of panel-local
WebSocket parsing. The frontend should fetch normalized HTTP resources, then
use a thin live bridge to update or invalidate query cache entries.

## Scope
- QueryClient configuration + sensible stale times
- Typed API client for /api/bootstrap, /api/phase, /api/agents, /api/beads,
  /api/prompts, /api/providers, /api/runs, /api/memory
- Feature hooks: usePhaseQuery(), useAgentsQuery(), useBeadsQuery(), etc.
- Mutation hooks for prompt-send, gate-advance, swarm-pause, swarm-resume
- useServerEvents() hook with reconnect backoff that patches/invalidate cache
- Dedicated log ring-buffer store for stdout.line events (do not put logs in Query)

## Why
A raw lastMessage hook would duplicate message parsing in every panel and make
reconnect/error behavior inconsistent. Query gives one cache, one loading/error
model, and one place to handle invalidation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- App bootstraps from HTTP without waiting for a WebSocket message
- Live events can refresh visible query data after mutations
- Mutation hooks return structured success/error states
- WebSocket reconnect does not wipe existing cached data
- stdout.line events bypass Query and land in a capped local buffer

## Files Created
- dashboard/src/api/client.ts
- dashboard/src/api/queryKeys.ts
- dashboard/src/hooks/useServerEvents.ts
- dashboard/src/providers/AppProviders.tsx

title: T11.3: PipelineView component (query-backed phase visualization + gate mutation)
description:
## What
React component showing the flywheel pipeline as a horizontal phase diagram
with the current phase highlighted and a gate advance action.

## Data Flow
- Read phase state from usePhaseQuery()
- Read current run summary from bootstrap or runs query
- Submit gate transitions via useMutation(POST /api/actions/gate-advance)
- Refresh from phase.updated or snapshot.updated live events

## UI Notes
- Completed phases: green checkmark
- Active phase: pulsing dot
- Future phases: muted circle
- Gate phases: amber lock with clear waiting state
- Advance button disabled unless the server reports a gated phase

## Acceptance Criteria
- No panel-local WebSocket message parsing
- Loading/error/empty states come from TanStack Query
- Gate action uses mutation lifecycle for pending/success/error UI
- Phase display refreshes after a successful advance

## Files Created
- dashboard/src/components/PipelineView.tsx

title: T11.4: AgentPanel component (query-backed agent status + prompt actions)
description:

React component showing one card per agent with status, pending mail, current
bead, and prompt-send controls.

## Data Flow
- Read agents from useAgentsQuery()
- Read prompts from usePromptsQuery() for the send control
- Submit prompt sends with useMutation(POST /api/actions/prompt-send)
- Refresh cards from snapshot.updated events or targeted cache updates

## UI Notes
- Cards grouped or filterable by agent type
- Pending send state should disable duplicate submits
- Server errors from prompt-send should render inline on the card or panel

## Acceptance Criteria
- Agent cards are driven by query data, not raw socket payloads
- Prompt send uses mutation lifecycle for pending/error/success UI
- Snapshot updates refresh cards without a full page reload

title: T11.5: BeadBoard component (query-backed kanban with derived stats)
description:
## What
React component showing beads in a kanban-style board with columns for
ready, in_progress, blocked, and closed, plus aggregate stats.

## Data Flow
- Read beads from useBeadsQuery()
- Prefer one normalized server payload that already includes counts and summary
- Refresh from beads.updated events or cache invalidation

## Design Decision
Do not force the kanban board itself into a table abstraction. Render cards
from query data directly; reserve TanStack Table for optional advanced
filtering/faceting work if the dataset grows.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Board renders from query data with no panel-specific WebSocket parsing
- Aggregate stats are derived consistently from the same source as the cards
- Bead updates propagate through cache refresh, not bespoke reducer logic

## Files Created
- dashboard/src/components/BeadBoard.tsx

title: T11.6: LogStream component (virtualized live output feed)
description:
## What
React component showing live agent stdout output with virtualized rendering
for performance. This bead keeps TanStack Virtual and deliberately avoids
routing high-frequency log lines through TanStack Query.

## Key Requirements
- Buffer cap: MAX_LINES = 1000 per session (drop oldest when exceeded)
- Use @tanstack/react-virtual for virtualized rendering
- Auto-scroll follows new output until the user scrolls away
- Session selector switches between NTM sessions
- stdout/stderr are visually distinct

## Data Flow
- Consume stdout.line events from the live bridge
- Store lines in a dedicated ring buffer keyed by session
- Do not put logs into React Query cache

## Acceptance Criteria
- 1000+ lines remain smooth during streaming
- Reconnect preserves prior buffered lines until replaced intentionally
- Log streaming does not trigger broad query cache churn

## Files Created
- dashboard/src/components/LogStream.tsx

title: T11.7: PromptLibrary component (TanStack Form browse + send workflow)
description:
## What
React component for browsing the prompt library and sending prompts to agents
with TanStack Form handling the send workflow.

## Layout
- Left: prompt list grouped by phase
- Right: prompt detail view (text, model, effort, phase)
- Bottom: send form (target selector, variables, send button)

## Data Flow
- Read prompts from usePromptsQuery()
- Read agents/providers/current run context from query hooks as needed
- Use TanStack Form for target selection, variable inputs, dirty state, and validation
- Submit with useMutation(POST /api/actions/prompt-send)

## Validation
- Highlight unresolved {variables} before submit
- Support broadcast, specific agent, and by-type targeting
- Render mutation errors inline without losing form state

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prompt sending uses TanStack Form instead of ad hoc local state
- Validation and submit lifecycle are centralized in the form layer
- Successful sends preserve a reliable audit-friendly payload to the server

## Files Created
- dashboard/src/components/PromptLibrary.tsx

title: T11.9: Optional TanStack Table row-model for bead/prompt filtering
description:
## What
Add TanStack Table only where it provides real leverage: advanced sorting,
filtering, faceting, and column visibility for large bead/prompt datasets.
This is explicitly optional and should not block the first dashboard slice.

## Candidate Uses
- A filterable side panel or drawer for beads

title: EPIC: SSH Layer
description:
# SSH Layer — Component 1: The Foundation

## Why This Exists
The entire console is a cockpit for a remote VPS. Every command, every status poll, every prompt send goes over SSH. Without a solid, well-abstracted SSH layer, everything else is built on sand.

## Architectural Decision: Single Persistent Connection
We use one persistent SSH connection (node-ssh wrapping ssh2) reused across all commands in a session. This avoids per-command handshake overhead and provides a single place to handle keepalive, timeout, and failure.

## Key Principle: Loud Failure, No Auto-Reconnect
The VPS keeps running autonomously whether or not the SSH session is alive. Agents don't stop. Beads don't stop. So when the SSH connection drops, we fail loudly with a red indicator and tell the user to run `flywheel ssh test`. We do NOT attempt auto-reconnection — that's a rabbit hole that hides real problems and introduces race conditions.

## What Goes Here
- SSHManager class (connect, exec, stream, dispose, isConnected)
- LineBuffer utility (SSH streams chunks, not lines — must buffer)
- bash -lc wrapper (non-interactive SSH strips PATH, tools are in ~/.local/bin)
- Connection logging to SQLite ssh_connections table

## What Does NOT Go Here
- NTM-specific logic (that's ntm-bridge.ts)
- Config loading (that's config.ts)
- Any reconnect logic

title: SSHManager class: connect(), dispose(), isConnected()
LineBuffer: chunk-to-line conversion utility
SSHManager.exec(): timeout, exit code, elapsed timing
SSHManager.stream(): AbortSignal, onLine callback, log tailing
Loud-fail guard: isConnected() check + user-facing error messages
SSH connection logging to sqlite (ssh_connections table)
description:
## What
Log every SSH connect/disconnect event to the ssh_connections table for diagnostics and latency tracking.

## Schema (already in state.ts)
ssh_connections: host, connected_at, disconnected_at, latency_ms

## Implementation
On successful connect: INSERT with connected_at + latency_ms (time to connect).
On dispose: UPDATE with disconnected_at.

## Why Track This
- Latency baseline helps diagnose VPS performance issues
- Connection history shows patterns (frequent disconnects = network instability)
- `flywheel ssh test` shows last 5 connection latencies

## Acceptance criteria
- Every connect() call inserts a row
- dispose() updates disconnected_at
- latency_ms is time from connect() call to ready

title: EPIC: Prompts YAML Completion

# Prompts YAML Completion

## Current State
prompts.yaml has 13 prompts. The pitch specifies 25+. The gap is mostly in swarm-phase management prompts and synthesis prompts for the wizard.

## Why Prompts Live in YAML (Not Code)
Every prompt is a policy decision. If Opus gets confused by swarm-init-briefing, you want to edit a YAML file and redeploy — not find the TypeScript, recompile, and rebundle. YAML makes prompts diffs, versionable, and editable by non-engineers.

## Variable Interpolation
Prompts support {variable} substitution. Variables are resolved from:
1. Explicit vars passed at call time
2. Current flywheel run context (SQLite)
3. VPS config (ssh.yaml remote_repo_root etc.)

## The 25+ Target
Target is a comprehensive playbook covering every phase transition and agent scenario. Think of it as the "ops manual" baked into the tool.

title: EPIC: Phase 1 Planning Wizard
description:
# Phase 1 Planning Wizard — The Highest-Leverage Piece

## The Core Bet
The wizard transforms a raw idea into a synthesis-grade planning packet through a structured, automated multi-model conversation. This is the piece that makes the console irreplaceable — you can't replicate this workflow manually without spending 2+ hours across 3 browser tabs.

## The Flow
1. User types idea
2. PARALLEL FAN-OUT: Claude Opus + GPT-4o + Gemini write competing plans simultaneously
3. Synthesis pass 1: Opus combines all three (diff-style revisions)
4. Synthesis pass 2: Opus refines synthesis (catch oversights)
5. Adversarial challenge: stress-test the plan (risks, open questions)
6. Brilliant ideas rounds x3 ("100 ideas, show me 10 best")
7. GATE: human reviews plan.md before proceeding to beads
8. Output: plan.md + wizard-log.jsonl saved to project dir

## Why Three Competing Models?
Each model has different training biases and knowledge cutoffs. GPT-4o tends toward pragmatic solutions. Gemini brings breadth. Opus brings depth and synthesis quality. The adversarial combination surface blind spots that a single-model pass misses.

## wizard-log.jsonl Schema (LOCKED — do not change without migration plan)
{"ts":"ISO8601","phase":"fan-out|synthesis-1|synthesis-2|adversarial|ideas-1|ideas-2|ideas-3","model":"model-id","prompt_hash":"sha256-first-8","input_tokens":N,"output_tokens":N,"elapsed_ms":N}

## CAAM Slot Rotation
CAAM has no standalone binary. Slot rotation is pure TypeScript round-robin in config.ts. The commit_slot from providers.yaml gets the commit agent. The swarm pool gets all others.

## --fast Mode Is Still Structured
--fast skips the parallel fan-out (no competing models) but keeps:
- One synthesis pass
- One brilliant ideas round
Output is still plan.md + wizard-log.jsonl. It is NOT a single raw prompt.

## Missing Dependency
@google/generative-ai is NOT in package.json. Must be added before any wizard work starts.

title: callModel() factory: unified interface for Anthropic + OpenAI + Gemini
Parallel fan-out: Promise.all with streaming progress display
description:
## What
Implement the fan-out step of the wizard: call 3 models simultaneously and stream their progress.

## Implementation
```typescript
async function fanOut(idea: string, slots: ModelSlot[]): Promise<string[]> {
  const labels = ['Model A (Opus)', 'Model B (GPT-4o)', 'Model C (Gemini)'];

  const results = await Promise.all(
    slots.map((slot, i) =>
      callModel(slot, planPrompt(idea), {
        onChunk: (text) => writeProgress(labels[i], text),
      })
    )
  );

  return results.map(r => r.text);
}
```

## Progress Display
Since all 3 models stream simultaneously, show interleaved progress:
```
[Model A] Analyzing the core problem...
[Model B] The main challenge here is...
[Model C] I'd approach this by...
[Model A] First, consider the data model...
```

Use chalk colors per model. Update in-place if terminal supports it (process.stdout.isTTY).

## Error Handling
If one model fails, save the others and throw with resume instructions:
```
Model B (GPT-4o) failed: Rate limit exceeded
Saved: Model A output to /tmp/wizard-fanout-A.txt
Saved: Model C output to /tmp/wizard-fanout-C.txt
Resume with: flywheel new "..." --skip-model gpt-4o --fanout-a /tmp/wizard-fanout-A.txt --fanout-c /tmp/wizard-fanout-C.txt
```

## Acceptance criteria
- Promise.all: all 3 models called simultaneously (not sequentially)
- Streaming progress shows per-model labels
- Partial failure saves completed outputs with resume instructions
- wizard-log.jsonl entry written for each model with tokens + elapsed

## wizard-log.jsonl
Complete log of all model turns, written to:
  ~/.flywheel/wizard-logs/<project_name>-<timestamp>.jsonl

Locked 7-field schema per line:
{"ts":"ISO8601","phase":"ideas-1|ideas-2|ideas-3","model":"model-id","prompt_hash":"sha256[:8]","input_tokens":N,"output_tokens":N,"elapsed_ms":N}


title: EPIC: T9 — Monitor + Local Server (HTTP API + live bridge + polling)
description:
## Overview
The local server runs on localhost:4200 and provides:
1. Stable HTTP API endpoints for dashboard reads
2. Safe action endpoints for the small set of permitted dashboard mutations
3. A read-only WebSocket live bridge for cache invalidation/patch events and stdout
4. Polling loops that query the VPS via SSH at configured intervals

This is the normalization layer between SSH/NTM/br and the dashboard. The
server owns the latest known snapshot so the frontend can bootstrap from HTTP
without waiting for a push event.

## Architecture
```
VPS (NTM, br) ←─SSH polling─→ Server (4200) ←─HTTP/WS─→ Dashboard (5173)
                                    ↓
                               SQLite (state)
```

## Polling Intervals (from pitch — non-negotiable)
- Agent status (--robot-snapshot): every 10s
- Bead state (br list/ready/stats): every 20s
- VPS health (disk/mem/load): every 45s
- Agent mail piggybacks on agent status

## Live Event Types
- snapshot.updated
- beads.updated
- phase.updated
- memory.updated
- stdout.line
- error

## Security: Loopback Only
Reject non-loopback HTTP and WebSocket access. This is a local dashboard, not
a remotely exposed control plane.

## Permitted Dashboard Mutations
- prompt-send
- swarm-pause
- swarm-resume
- gate-advance
No other write operations from the dashboard.

## Files
- cli/server.ts (HTTP + WS server)
- cli/monitor.ts (polling loop orchestration)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Server starts on localhost:4200
- HTTP endpoints return normalized JSON snapshots
- Live bridge emits typed events for cache refresh + stdout streaming
- Loopback-only enforcement works
- Only the permitted mutation endpoints are exposed

title: T9.1: Local server API contract (normalized HTTP reads + safe action endpoints)
description:
## What
Implement the local server in cli/server.ts using Node http + ws packages,
with HTTP as the dashboard source of truth and WebSocket as a read-only live
bridge.

## HTTP Reads
- GET /api/bootstrap
- GET /api/phase
- GET /api/agents
- GET /api/beads
- GET /api/prompts
- GET /api/providers
- GET /api/runs
- GET /api/memory

## HTTP Actions
- POST /api/actions/prompt-send
- POST /api/actions/gate-advance
- POST /api/actions/swarm-pause
- POST /api/actions/swarm-resume

Each action returns structured success/error JSON that can map cleanly to
TanStack Query mutation states.

## WebSocket Live Bridge
- Mount on /ws
- Loopback check on upgrade
- Read-only push events for cache invalidation/patches and stdout lines
- No panel-specific bespoke payloads

## Acceptance Criteria
- Server starts on port 4200
- HTTP routes return stable normalized JSON
- Action endpoints reject unknown operations and malformed payloads clearly
- WebSocket accepts loopback clients only
- Bootstrap route is sufficient for first-paint dashboard render

## Files Modified

title: T9.2: Monitor polling + live cache bridge (SSH → normalized state → events)
description:
## What
Implement cli/monitor.ts as the polling/orchestration layer that fetches data
from SSH/NTM/br, keeps the latest normalized server-side snapshot warm, and
emits query-friendly live events.

## Responsibilities
- Poll NTM snapshot every 10s
- Poll beads state every 20s
- Poll VPS health every 45s
- Optionally derive phase/memory summaries from DB or tool output
- Write latest results into an in-memory cache or server-owned state object
- Broadcast compact events such as snapshot.updated, beads.updated, phase.updated
- Stream stdout.line separately from structured state refresh events

## Design Rules
- Each poll path is isolated; one failure must not stop the others
- HTTP reads should serve the latest cached state without re-running SSH work
- Event payloads should be stable enough for targeted cache patching or invalidation

## Acceptance Criteria
- Polls run at the specified intervals
- HTTP routes can read from the latest cached server state
- Live events are emitted when server-side state changes
- stdout streaming remains separate from structured snapshot refreshes
- Individual poll failures surface as error events without killing the loop

## Files Modified
- cli/monitor.ts (new)

title: T9.3: flywheel serve command (start server + open dashboard)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel serve — starts the HTTP/WS server on 4200 and optionally
the Vite dev server for the dashboard on 5173.

## Flow
1. Load all configs (ssh, providers, prompts)
2. Initialize DB
3. Connect SSH
4. Initialize NtmBridge, PromptEngine, GateMachine
5. Start Monitor polling loop
6. Start HTTP + WS server on 4200
7. If dashboard/ exists, spawn Vite dev server on 5173
8. Print: "Dashboard: http://localhost:5173 | API: http://localhost:4200"
9. Handle SIGINT for graceful shutdown

## Graceful Shutdown
On SIGINT:
1. Stop monitor polling
2. Close all WebSocket connections
3. Close HTTP server
4. Dispose SSH connection
5. Close SQLite (WAL checkpoint)
6. Kill Vite process if spawned

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/serve.ts (new — or integrate into server.ts)
- cli/index.ts (wire up)

title: T9.4: flywheel monitor (terminal-mode live view, no browser)

title: EPIC: Vite Dashboard
description:
# Vite Dashboard — The Monitoring Glass

## What It Is
A locally-served web UI (localhost:5173 in dev, localhost:4200/dashboard in prod) that gives a live view of flywheel state. It connects to the local flywheel server over WebSocket — NOT directly to the VPS.

## Six Panels
1. PipelineView: Phase pipeline + gate advance button
2. BeadBoard: Kanban with blocked/todo/done counts, critical path highlight
3. AgentPanel: Per-agent type, state, pending_mail, current bead
4. PromptLibrary: Browse all 25+ prompts, send to agent or all
5. LogStream: Live stdout feed from agents (virtualized, auto-scroll)
6. MemoryPanel: cass/cm session memory browser

## Key Implementation Notes
- Log buffer capped at 1000 lines per session (prevents memory leak)
- @tanstack/react-virtual for LogStream (DOM can't handle 10k lines)
- useWebSocket hook must reconnect with exponential backoff
- Shared TypeScript types between server and dashboard (no code gen)

## Mutation Boundary
The dashboard can ONLY do 3 things that change state:
1. Send a prompt
2. Pause/resume swarm
3. Advance a gate
Everything else is read-only display.

title: Init Vite + React + Tailwind + @tanstack/react-virtual
useWebSocket() + useSessionFeed() hooks

title: End-to-end integration test (full flywheel run against test project)
description:
## What
Verify the complete flywheel pipeline works end-to-end before shipping.

## Test Scenario
1. `flywheel settings ssh` — configure VPS (use existing acfs_ed25519 key)
2. `flywheel preflight` — verify all tools present
3. `flywheel new "Build a simple todo CLI in TypeScript"` — run wizard in --fast mode
4. `flywheel beads generate` — generate beads from plan.md
5. `flywheel swarm 2` — spawn 2 agents
6. Watch dashboard for 10 minutes
7. `flywheel gate advance` — advance through swarm → review
8. `flywheel review --passes 1`
9. `flywheel gate advance` → deploy gate
10. Verify phase_events log in SQLite shows complete history

## What To Verify
- All phase transitions recorded in phase_events
- Bead snapshots written every 20s in bead_snapshots
- API costs logged in api_calls
- Dashboard shows real agent state
- Commit agent made at least one commit
- wizard-log.jsonl has correct schema

## This Bead Blocks Release
Do not mark the project done until this integration test passes.

## Acceptance criteria
- Full pipeline runs without manual intervention (except gate advance)
- All 7 SQLite tables have data after the run
- Dashboard updates in real time throughout
- No SSH connection drops during 10-minute swarm

title: EPIC: T10 — Review + Deploy + Beads Commands
description:
## Overview
The final phase commands: review (multi-pass agent review), deploy (gated
deployment), and beads (bead management from the console).

## Commands
- flywheel review [--passes <n>] — orchestrate review rounds
- flywheel deploy — gated deployment with explicit confirmation
- flywheel beads generate|refine|triage|history — bead management

## Review Flow
For each pass (default 2):
1. Broadcast fresh-review prompt to all agents
2. Wait for idle (agents have read and responded)
3. Broadcast peer-review prompt (cross-review)
4. Wait for idle
5. Run ubs scan remotely, capture output
6. Store results in phase_events

## Deploy Safety
- Requires user to type "DEPLOY <project-name>" (no -y flag, ever)
- Asserts checkpoint_sha is set (from swarm phase)
- Runs deploy command on VPS (gh workflow run or custom)
- Logs to phase_events

## Files
- cli/commands/review.ts
- cli/commands/deploy.ts
- cli/commands/beads.ts

## Acceptance Criteria
- review runs N passes with fresh + peer review prompts
- review includes ubs scan results
- deploy requires explicit typed confirmation
- beads commands proxy to br on VPS via SSH

title: T10.1: flywheel review command (multi-pass review orchestration)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel review [--passes <n>] — orchestrates N review passes
where agents perform fresh + peer review cycles.

## Flow Per Pass
1. Assert gate at REVIEW phase
2. Broadcast fresh-review prompt to all agents
3. Wait for idle (IdleDetector from T8.4)
4. Broadcast peer-review prompt (cross-review between agents)
5. Wait for idle
6. Run ubs scan on VPS: bash -lc "cd <project> && ubs"
7. Capture and store ubs output in phase_events

## After All Passes
- Print review summary: N passes, findings count
- Run security-audit prompt if Opus available
- Advance gate to GATE_REVIEW

## --passes Default
Default is 2 passes. More passes → more thorough but more cost.

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/review.ts (new)
- cli/index.ts (wire up)

title: T10.2: flywheel deploy (gated deployment with typed confirmation)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel deploy — the final deployment command with strict safety.

## Safety Contract (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
- User MUST type "DEPLOY <project-name>" to confirm
- NO -y flag, NO --yes flag, NO skip confirmation
- This is the most destructive command in the console

## Flow
1. Assert gate at DEPLOY phase
2. Assert checkpoint_sha is set in flywheel_runs
3. Print deployment summary:
   ```
   Deploy "habit-tracker"
   Commit: abc123..def456 (47 files changed)
   Review: 2 passes, 0 critical findings
   Cost: $15.23

   Type "DEPLOY habit-tracker" to confirm:
   ```
4. Read user input, validate exact match
5. Run deploy command on VPS (configurable):
   - Default: gh workflow run deploy
   - Custom: whatever is in project config
6. Log to phase_events
7. Advance gate to DONE
8. Print: "✓ Deployed. Run ID #3 complete."

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/deploy.ts (new)
- cli/index.ts (wire up)

title: EPIC: Core Commands
description:
# Core Commands — The User-Facing CLI Surface

## What Lives Here
The commands a human actually types:
- flywheel settings ssh (interactive config wizard)
- flywheel ssh test (connection + tool version matrix)
- flywheel preflight [--force] (verify VPS environment)
- flywheel prompts list / send
- flywheel providers (slot usage)
- flywheel doctor (full diagnostic)

## Design Principle: stdout is JSON, stderr is human
All machine-readable output goes to stdout. All progress/status messages go to stderr. This lets you pipe `flywheel ssh test --json` into other tools without parsing log noise.

## Exit Code Contract (preflight)
0 = all required checks passed
2 = warnings present, continued with --force
3 = required check failed, hard stop
Required tools: ntm, br, git, gh
Optional (warn only): bv, ubs, dcg, cass

title: flywheel settings ssh — interactive SSH config wizard
description:
## What
Implement `flywheel settings ssh` — an interactive wizard that creates/updates ~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml.

## UX
```
$ flywheel settings ssh
? VPS host: [217.216.93.175]
? SSH user: [ubuntu]
? SSH port: [22]
? Private key path: [~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519]
? Remote repo root: [/home/ubuntu/projects]
Testing connection... ✓ Connected (24ms)
Saved to ~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml
```

## Implementation
Use Node.js `readline` (built-in, no dep). Show existing values as defaults in brackets.
After saving, immediately call `flywheel ssh test` to validate.

## File Creation
```typescript
import { createInterface } from 'readline/promises';
const rl = createInterface({ input: process.stdin, output: process.stdout });
const host = await rl.question(`VPS host [${existing?.host ?? ''}]: `);
```

## Acceptance criteria
- Existing values shown as defaults (re-run doesn't wipe config)
- Connection test runs automatically after save
- File permissions set to 600 on create
- Clear error if key file doesn't exist

title: flywheel ssh test — connection + tool version matrix
description:
## What
Implement `flywheel ssh test` — verifies SSH connection and checks all required tools.

## Output
```
✓ Connected to ubuntu@217.216.93.175 (port 22) — 24ms
✓ ntm v1.2.0
✓ br v0.x.x
✓ bv available
✓ ubs available
✓ dcg available
✓ cass available
✓ gh v2.x.x
✓ git v2.x.x
```

## Implementation
For each tool, run: `which <tool> && <tool> --version 2>&1 | head -1`
Parse version string best-effort. Missing tool shows ✗ in red.

## --json flag
```json
{
  "connected": true,
  "latency_ms": 24,
  "tools": {
    "ntm": { "available": true, "version": "1.2.0" },
    "br": { "available": true, "version": "0.x.x" }
  }
}
```

## Acceptance criteria
- Exits 0 if connection succeeds (even if optional tools missing)
- Exits 1 if SSH connection fails
- --json flag outputs machine-readable result
- Latency logged to ssh_connections table

title: flywheel preflight [--force] — exit code contract
description:
## What
Implement `flywheel preflight` — environment verification with hard-coded exit code contract.

## Exit Code Contract
- 0: all required checks passed
- 2: warnings present, continued with --force
- 3: required check failed, hard stop

## Required Tools (hard fail without --force)
ntm, br, git, gh

## Optional Tools (warn, continue)
bv, ubs, dcg, cass, agent-mail

## Additional Checks
- ~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml exists and is valid
- ~/.flywheel/providers.yaml exists and is valid (warn if missing)
- VPS disk usage < 90%
- NTM can connect to tmux

## Implementation
```typescript
const checks: Check[] = [
  { name: 'ntm', required: true, cmd: 'which ntm' },
  { name: 'br', required: true, cmd: 'which br' },
  // ...
];
const failures = checks.filter(c => !run(c));
const required = failures.filter(c => c.required);
if (required.length && !opts.force) process.exit(3);
if (failures.length && opts.force) process.exit(2);
process.exit(0);
```

## Acceptance criteria
- exit(3) on missing ntm/br/git/gh without --force
- exit(2) on missing optional tools with --force
- exit(0) when all required tools present
- Each check printed with ✓ or ✗ and tool name

title: flywheel prompts list + flywheel prompts send
description:
## What
Implement the two prompts commands.

## flywheel prompts list
```
PHASE    NAME                      MODEL    EFFORT
───────────────────────────────────────────────────
plan     generate-ideas            opus     max
plan     plan-adversarial-...      opus     max
plan     plan-synthesis-1          opus     max
beads    beads-generate-from-plan  opus     max
beads    beads-review-refine       opus     max
swarm    swarm-init-briefing       any      high
...
```
Group by phase. Truncate long names to 25 chars. Color-code by phase.

## flywheel prompts send <name> [--agent <pane>] [--all] [--var key=val]
1. Load prompt from prompts.yaml
2. Interpolate variables (from --var flags + run context from SQLite)
3. Call PromptEngine.send() (→ NtmBridge.send())
4. Log to prompt_sends table
5. Print result: which agents received it, any failures

## Variable Override
--var plan_path=/home/ubuntu/projects/myapp/plan.md
Multiple --var flags supported.

## Acceptance criteria
- prompts list shows all 25+ prompts grouped by phase
- prompts send fails clearly if prompt name not found
- prompts send with missing required variable throws (not silently sends {plan_path})
- Sends are logged to prompt_sends table with run_id if active run exists

title: flywheel providers + flywheel doctor
description:
## What
Two diagnostic commands.

## flywheel providers
Shows current slot configuration and rotation state:
```
SLOT        MODEL              PROVIDER    CONCURRENT   NEXT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
plan[0]     claude-opus-4-6    anthropic   1            ✓ (next)
plan[1]     gpt-4o             openai      1
plan[2]     gemini-2.0         google      1
synthesis   claude-opus-4-6    anthropic   1
swarm[0]    claude-sonnet-4-6  anthropic   2            ✓ (next)
swarm[1]    claude-sonnet-4-6  anthropic   2
commit      claude-sonnet-4-6  anthropic   1
```

## flywheel doctor
Comprehensive diagnostic that checks:
- SSH connectivity and latency
- All tool presence and versions
- Config file validity and permissions
- SQLite DB health (WAL mode, file size, row counts)
- VPS disk/memory/load
- Active tmux sessions
- Beads workspace state

Outputs a structured report with PASS/WARN/FAIL per check.

## Acceptance criteria
- providers shows rotation index (which model is "next" in round-robin)
- doctor exits 0 if all required checks pass
- doctor --json outputs structured report for programmatic use

title: EPIC: SQLite + Config Layer
description:
# SQLite + Config Layer — The Data Foundation

## Why This Exists
The console needs persistent state across CLI invocations:
- Which phase is the current run in?
- Which prompts were sent and when?
- How much have we spent on API calls this run?
- What was the bead state 10 minutes ago?

SQLite (better-sqlite3, synchronous) is the right choice: no daemon, single file, ACID, fast.

## Config Architecture
Three separate YAML files, each with a distinct security posture:
- ssh.yaml: VPS connection settings (chmod 600, no API keys)
- providers.yaml: Model keys + CAAM slots (chmod 600, separate from SSH)
- prompts.yaml: Prompt library (not sensitive, can be committed)

These are intentionally separate so you can share prompts.yaml in git without exposing credentials.

## Zod Validation
Every config file is validated with Zod on load. ALL errors are surfaced at once (safeParse, not parse) so the user doesn't fix one field and get hit with another error.

## WAL Mode Is Non-Negotiable
journal_mode=WAL is set immediately after every db.open(). This ensures reads don't block writes and vice versa — critical when the polling server and CLI commands share the same DB file.

## Seven Tables (defined in state.ts, already scaffolded)
wizard_runs, flywheel_runs, ssh_connections, prompt_sends, phase_events, bead_snapshots, api_calls

title: initDb(): WAL mode, schema creation, idempotent
Prepared statement classes: EventLog, BeadSnapshots
Prepared statements: ApiCallLog + PromptSendLog
description:
## What
Prepared statement wrappers for api_calls and prompt_sends tables.

## ApiCallLog
Tracks every LLM API call: model, phase, tokens, cost_usd.
Cost is calculated from providers.yaml pricing table:
  cost = (input_tokens / 1_000_000 * input_per_1m) + (output_tokens / 1_000_000 * output_per_1m)

## PromptSendLog
Audit trail for every prompt injection:
  prompt_name, agent_target, sent_at, run_id

## Why Track API Costs
The wizard fan-out calls 3 models simultaneously. Without cost tracking, a single wizard run could burn $50 unnoticed. The budget guard in swarm phase also reads from api_calls.

## Acceptance criteria
- ApiCallLog.record() computes cost correctly from pricing table
- PromptSendLog.record() is called by PromptEngine.send()
- Both classes exported from cli/state.ts

loadProviders(): Zod schema + slot validation + env interpolation

EPIC: Swarm + Gate Machine
description:
# Swarm + Gate Machine — Phase Orchestration

## The Gate Concept
Between every phase transition there is a human gate. The console pauses and waits for `flywheel gate advance`. This is the safety net that prevents autonomous runaway. Gates can be bypassed with --autopilot, but that is opt-in, not default.

## Phase State Machine
INIT → PLAN → GATE_PLAN → BEADS → GATE_BEADS → SWARM → GATE_SWARM → REVIEW → GATE_REVIEW → DEPLOY → DONE
All transitions are recorded in phase_events (append-only, source of truth for replay).

## Commit Agent Safety (Non-Negotiable Constraints)
The commit agent fires every 15-20 minutes during swarm. Its safety constraints are injected into its init prompt and are NOT configurable:
- MAY: git add already-tracked files, git commit, git push
- MAY NOT: rebase, merge, reset --hard, checkout ., run deploys, git add untracked files

## Idle Detection
Byte-identical snapshots x3 at 5s intervals (15s total) → declare agent idle.
Do NOT parse Claude Code output text — too fragile. Compare output_tail_lines counts from --robot-snapshot.

## Budget Guard
Track cumulative api_calls.cost_usd per run_id. If --budget <usd> is set, stop swarm when exceeded.

title: Phase state machine: enum + transition table
description:
## What
Define the phase state machine that drives the flywheel.

## Phase Enum
```typescript
export enum Phase {
  INIT = 'init',
  PLAN = 'plan',
  GATE_PLAN = 'gate_plan',
  BEADS = 'beads',
  GATE_BEADS = 'gate_beads',
  SWARM = 'swarm',
  GATE_SWARM = 'gate_swarm',
  REVIEW = 'review',
  GATE_REVIEW = 'gate_review',
  DEPLOY = 'deploy',
  DONE = 'done',
  FAILED = 'failed',
}
```

## Transition Table
```typescript
const TRANSITIONS: Record<Phase, Phase | null> = {
  [Phase.INIT]:        Phase.PLAN,
  [Phase.PLAN]:        Phase.GATE_PLAN,
  [Phase.GATE_PLAN]:   Phase.BEADS,
  [Phase.BEADS]:       Phase.GATE_BEADS,
  [Phase.GATE_BEADS]:  Phase.SWARM,
  [Phase.SWARM]:       Phase.GATE_SWARM,
  [Phase.GATE_SWARM]:  Phase.REVIEW,
  [Phase.REVIEW]:      Phase.GATE_REVIEW,
  [Phase.GATE_REVIEW]: Phase.DEPLOY,
  [Phase.DEPLOY]:      Phase.DONE,
  [Phase.DONE]:        null,   // terminal
  [Phase.FAILED]:      null,   // terminal
};
```

## Why GATE Phases Are Separate States
Having GATE_* as explicit states (not booleans) means:
- The gate state survives process restarts (stored in SQLite)
- `flywheel gate status` can show exactly what's waiting
- phase_events log captures when gates open/close
- Autopilot mode can skip gate states cleanly

## Acceptance criteria
- Phase enum exported from cli/gate-machine.ts
- TRANSITIONS table is complete and has no cycles
- Each transition is recorded in phase_events (append-only)

title: GateMachine class: advance(), waitForGate(), currentPhase()
flywheel swarm <count> command
description:
## What
Implement `flywheel swarm <count> [--no-commit] [--budget <usd>]`

## Flow
1. Assert current phase == GATE_BEADS (gate must be open)
2. Validate beads workspace exists on VPS (br info)
3. Generate session name: flywheel-<project>-<timestamp>
4. Spawn <count> Claude agents via NtmBridge.spawn()
5. If not --no-commit: spawn 1 additional Claude agent as commit agent
6. Wait for all agents to be ready (--spawn-wait)
7. Send swarm-init-briefing to all non-commit agents
8. Send commit agent its constrained init prompt
9. Advance gate: GATE_BEADS → SWARM
10. Start monitoring loop (T9 server)
11. Print dashboard URL + session status

## Session Naming
flywheel-<project_name>-<yyyymmdd-hhmmss>
Max 40 chars for tmux compatibility.

## Acceptance criteria
- Fails with clear error if phase != GATE_BEADS
- Commit agent spawned in separate pane with constrained prompt
- flywheel_runs table updated with session_name
- Budget stored in flywheel_runs.notes as JSON {"budget_usd": N}

title: Commit agent provisioning + safety constraint injection
description:
## What
Provision the commit agent with its mandatory safety constraints injected directly into the init prompt.

## The Commit Agent Role
Fires every 15-20 minutes during swarm. Commits and pushes all changed (already-tracked) files.
This is a first-class agent role, not optional plumbing.

## Safety Constraints (INJECTED IN PROMPT — NOT CONFIGURABLE)
The commit agent MUST receive these constraints in its init prompt:

```
You are the commit agent for this flywheel swarm. Your sole job is to periodically
commit and push work that other agents have done.

STRICT RULES — violating any of these will cause immediate session termination:
✓ You MAY run: git status, git diff, git add <tracked-file>, git commit -m "...", git push
✗ You MUST NOT run: git rebase, git merge, git reset, git checkout ., git clean, git push --force
✗ You MUST NOT: run tests, deploy, modify CI config, git add untracked files
✗ You MUST NOT: install packages, modify package.json, touch .env files

Commit schedule: every 15-20 minutes. Commit message format: "chore: agent commit [timestamp]"
```

## CAAM Slot Policy
Use providers.slots.commit_slot if configured.
Fall back to providers.slots.swarm[0] if commit_slot is not set.

## Acceptance criteria
- Commit agent always gets constrained prompt (not generic swarm-init-briefing)
- Constraint text is in a YAML template (not hardcoded TypeScript)
- commit_slot → swarm[0] fallback works
- Commit agent pane labeled in NTM session as "commit"

title: Idle detection + budget guard
flywheel gate advance + flywheel gate status + flywheel rollback
description:
## What
The human gate control commands.

## flywheel gate status
```
Current run: flywheel-myapp-20260309-140522
Phase: gate_swarm
Waiting for human to advance gate.

Swarm summary:
  Active agents: 4/5
  Idle agents: 1 (agent-2, 8min)
  Beads done: 18/42 (43%)
  Estimated completion: ~45min

Run 'flywheel gate advance' to proceed to review phase.
```

## flywheel gate advance
```typescript
program.command('gate advance')
  .option('--run <id>', 'Run ID (defaults to most recent active run)')
  .action(async (opts) => {
    const runId = opts.run ?? db.prepare('SELECT id FROM flywheel_runs WHERE phase LIKE "gate_%" ORDER BY started_at DESC LIMIT 1').get()?.id;
    const next = gate.advance(runId);
    console.log(chalk.green(`Gate advanced → ${next}`));
  });
```

## flywheel rollback [run-id]
Resets a run to a checkpoint_sha:
1. `git reset --hard <checkpoint_sha>` on VPS (via SSH)
2. Update flywheel_runs.phase to 'init'
3. Log rollback event to phase_events

WARNING: Rollback is destructive. Require explicit confirmation:
"Type ROLLBACK <run-id> to confirm: "

## Acceptance criteria
- gate status shows phase, bead progress, idle agents
- gate advance fails if no active run in a GATE_* phase
- rollback requires explicit typed confirmation
- rollback logs to phase_events before executing git reset

title: EPIC: T3 — Prompts YAML Library (25+ Phase-Tagged Prompts)
description:
## Overview
Complete the prompt library from its current ~14 prompts to 25+ prompts
covering all 5 flywheel phases. This is the central knowledge base that
powers the entire orchestration — every agent interaction is routed through
a named, versioned prompt with model/effort metadata.

## Architecture
- Single file: config/prompts.yaml
- Each prompt has: text, model, effort, phase
- text supports {variable} interpolation (resolved at send time)
- model can be: opus, gpt-4o, gemini, any
- effort can be: max, high, medium, low
- phase can be: plan, beads, swarm, review, deploy

## Why a YAML Library (not hardcoded strings)
1. Version-controlled alongside code (git diff shows prompt changes)
2. Non-developers can review/edit prompts without touching TypeScript
3. The dashboard (T11) PromptLibrary panel reads directly from this
4. Easy to A/B test by swapping prompt text without code changes
5. Model routing: prompt specifies preferred model, engine routes it

## Current State (verified 2026-03-10)
config/prompts.yaml has ~14 prompts covering plan and review phases.
Missing: swarm phase prompts, deploy phase, memory/cass prompts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- ≥25 prompts in prompts.yaml
- All 5 phases represented (plan, beads, swarm, review, deploy)
- All prompts have text, model, effort, phase fields
- {variable} placeholders documented in comments
- flywheel prompts list shows all prompts grouped by phase

title: T3.1: Add swarm-phase prompts (init-briefing, claim, complete, check-mail, conflict)
description:
## What
Add 5 new prompts to config/prompts.yaml for the swarm phase.

## Prompts to Add

### swarm-init-briefing
- phase: swarm, model: any, effort: high
- Sent to each agent at swarm start
- Contains: project context, bead assignments, commit rules, agent-mail instructions

### bead-claim

title: EPIC: T2 — SQLite + Config Layer (State Foundation)
description:
## Overview
The state layer provides persistent storage (SQLite) and configuration loading
(YAML + Zod) for the entire console. Every command reads config; most commands
write to SQLite for audit trail and cost tracking.

## Architecture Decisions

### SQLite with WAL mode
- better-sqlite3 is synchronous — perfect for CLI where we want blocking writes
- WAL mode allows concurrent reads during writes
- PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL trades tiny durability risk for 2-3x write speed
- Foreign keys ON for referential integrity
- Checkpoint on SIGTERM to flush WAL

### Config Files (not in SQLite)
- ssh.yaml → SSH connection parameters (sensitive)
- providers.yaml → API keys + model slots (sensitive, chmod 600)
- prompts.yaml → prompt library (version-controlled)
- All loaded with js-yaml + Zod validation

### No ORM
Raw SQL with prepared statements. The schema is 7 tables with simple queries.
An ORM would add complexity and hide what's happening. Prepared statements
give us type safety via TypeScript generics on better-sqlite3.

## Files
- cli/state.ts (SQLite init + prepared statement managers)
- cli/config.ts (all YAML loaders — extends T1.1)

## Schema (7 tables)
1. wizard_runs — Phase 1 planning sessions
2. flywheel_runs — Full flywheel run tracking
3. ssh_connections — Connection audit log
4. prompt_sends — Prompt injection history
5. phase_events — Append-only event log (the core audit trail)
6. bead_snapshots — Periodic bead state captures
7. api_calls — Token usage + cost tracking

## Acceptance Criteria
- initDb() idempotent — safe to call on existing DB
- All config loaders use Zod safeParse (show all errors at once)
- WAL checkpoint on SIGTERM
- Missing config files → clear error with instructions
- providers.yaml chmod 600 check (warn if world-readable)

title: T2.1: SQLite schema initialization (initDb + 7 tables + WAL)
T2.2: Prepared statement managers (EventLog, ApiCallLog, etc.)
T2.3: providers.yaml loader + Zod schema + slot rotation
T2.4: FlywheelRun manager (create/update/query runs)
description:
## What
Implement FlywheelRunLog manager — the core tracking object for a full
flywheel execution (plan → beads → swarm → review → deploy).

## Why
Every flywheel command operates in the context of a "run". The run tracks:
- Current phase and gate state
- Cumulative cost
- Checkpoint SHA (for rollback)
- Start/end timestamps

## Key Methods
- create(projectName, idea): number — returns run ID
- updatePhase(runId, phase): void
- setCheckpointSha(runId, sha): void
- addCost(runId, amount): void
- complete(runId): void
- getCurrent(): FlywheelRun | null — most recent incomplete run
- getById(runId): FlywheelRun

title: EPIC: T6 — Phase 1 Planning Wizard (Multi-Model Plan Generation)
description:
## Overview
The Phase 1 Wizard is the highest-leverage component: it takes a raw idea and
produces a structured plan.md through a multi-model fan-out → synthesis →
adversarial → brilliant-ideas pipeline. This is the entry point for every new
project in the flywheel.

## Pipeline (Full Mode)
```
User Idea
  ↓
Fan-out: 3 models simultaneously (Opus, GPT-4o, Gemini)
  ↓
Synthesis Pass 1: Merge 3 plans (Opus)
  ↓
Synthesis Pass 2: Catch oversights (Opus)
  ↓
Adversarial Challenge: Attack the plan (Opus)
  ↓
Brilliant Ideas × 3 rounds: Enhance with insights (Opus)
  ↓
Output: plan.md + wizard-log.jsonl
```

## Pipeline (Fast Mode — --fast flag)
```
User Idea → Single model → One synthesis → One ideas round → Output
```

## Cost Tracking
Every API call logged to api_calls table with:
- model, input_tokens, output_tokens
- cost_usd (computed from providers.yaml pricing table)
- phase ("plan"), called_at

## Output Artifacts
1. plan.md — The structured implementation plan
2. wizard-log.jsonl — Append-only log of every API call:
   {"ts":"...","phase":"fan-out","model":"claude-opus-4-6","prompt_hash":"...","input_tokens":1200,"output_tokens":3400,"elapsed_ms":8200}

## Error Strategy
If one fan-out model fails (e.g., GPT-4o rate limit), save the successful ones
and fail loudly: "Model B (GPT-4o) failed: <error>. Run with --skip-model gpt-4o."
Never silently degrade to fewer models.

## Files
- cli/commands/new.ts (command handler)
- cli/wizard.ts (pipeline orchestration)
- cli/models.ts (multi-model API client)

## Acceptance Criteria
- flywheel new "idea" produces plan.md + wizard-log.jsonl
- flywheel new "idea" --fast skips fan-out, uses single model
- Cost tracked in SQLite for every API call
- --push-artifacts SCPs outputs to VPS
- Partial fan-out failure → clear error with --skip-model hint

title: T6.1: Multi-model API client (Anthropic + OpenAI + Google)
T6.2: Wizard pipeline orchestration (fan-out → synthesis → adversarial → ideas)
description:
## What
Implement cli/wizard.ts — the core pipeline that orchestrates the multi-pass
planning process from raw idea to structured plan.

## Pipeline Steps (Full Mode)

### Step 1: Parallel Fan-Out
```typescript
const [opusPlan, gptPlan, geminiPlan] = await Promise.all([
  callModel(planSlots[0], planPrompt(idea), { onChunk: streamProgress('Model A') }),
  callModel(planSlots[1], planPrompt(idea), { onChunk: streamProgress('Model B') }),
  callModel(planSlots[2], planPrompt(idea), { onChunk: streamProgress('Model C') }),
]);
```
- Uses plan slots from providers.yaml (up to 3 competing models)
- Streams progress to terminal with model labels
- If a model fails, catch the error and store null (handle in step 2)

### Step 2: Synthesis Pass 1
- Merges successful plans using plan-synthesis prompt
- If only 2/3 plans succeeded, synthesis adapts
- Always uses synthesis slot (Opus)

### Step 3: Synthesis Pass 2 (Refine)
- Takes synthesis-1 output and catches oversights
- Uses plan-refine prompt
- Always uses synthesis slot (Opus)

### Step 4: Adversarial Challenge
- Attacks the refined plan: what could go wrong? What is missing?
- Uses plan-adversarial-challenge prompt (already in prompts.yaml)
- The plan is updated based on adversarial feedback

### Step 5: Brilliant Ideas × 3 Rounds
- Three rounds of "brilliant ideas" enhancement
- Each round builds on the previous: the plan accumulates novel insights
- Uses generate-ideas prompt (already in prompts.yaml)

### Output
Returns: { plan: string, runId: number, wizardLog: WizardLogEntry[] }

## Pipeline Steps (Fast Mode — --fast)
Skip fan-out, use single model: Draft → Synthesis → 1 Ideas Round

## Streaming Progress Display
Show concurrent progress in terminal:
```
Model A (claude-opus-4-6): ████████░░ 3,400 tokens
Model B (gpt-4o):          ██████░░░░ 2,100 tokens
Model C (gemini-1.5-pro):  ████░░░░░░ 1,200 tokens
```
Use chalk + cursor movement for live update.

## Error Handling
Fan-out partial failure:
- 3/3 succeed → normal synthesis
- 2/3 succeed → synthesis with note about missing model
- 1/3 succeed → proceed with single plan, skip synthesis
- 0/3 succeed → hard fail

## Acceptance Criteria
- Full mode: 3 fan-out → 2 synthesis → 1 adversarial → 3 ideas rounds
- Fast mode: 1 draft → 1 synthesis → 1 ideas round
- Streaming progress shows all models concurrently
- Partial fan-out failure handled gracefully with clear message
- Every API call logged via wizard-log.jsonl + api_calls table

## Files Modified
- cli/wizard.ts (new)

title: T6.3: Wizard output (plan.md + wizard-log.jsonl generation)
description:
## What
Generate the final output artifacts from the wizard pipeline:
1. plan.md — structured implementation plan
2. wizard-log.jsonl — append-only API call log

## plan.md Format
The final plan text from the brilliant-ideas rounds, written as Markdown.
Should include:
- Project name and idea (from user input)
- Generated timestamp
- Plan body (from pipeline output)
- Model attribution (which models contributed)

## wizard-log.jsonl Schema (LOCKED — do not modify)
Each line is a JSON object with exactly these 7 fields:
```json
{"ts":"2026-03-09T12:00:00Z","phase":"fan-out","model":"claude-opus-4-6","prompt_hash":"abc123","input_tokens":1200,"output_tokens":3400,"elapsed_ms":8200}
```

Fields:
- ts: ISO 8601 timestamp
- phase: pipeline step name (fan-out, synthesis-1, synthesis-2, adversarial, ideas-1, ideas-2, ideas-3)
- model: exact model ID used
- prompt_hash: SHA-256 hash of prompt text (first 12 chars)
- input_tokens: token count from API response
- output_tokens: token count from API response
- elapsed_ms: wall clock time for the API call

### prompt_hash
Hash the exact prompt text sent to the API. This allows tracking prompt changes
across wizard runs without storing the full text in the log.

## --push-artifacts Flag
When --push-artifacts is set:
1. SCP plan.md to ubuntu@host:~/projects/<name>/plan.md
2. SCP wizard-log.jsonl to ubuntu@host:~/projects/<name>/wizard-log.jsonl
3. Print: "✓ Artifacts pushed to VPS"

## Acceptance Criteria
- plan.md written to local ~/.flywheel/runs/<runId>/plan.md
- wizard-log.jsonl matches the locked 7-field schema exactly
- --push-artifacts SCPs both files to VPS
- wizard_runs table updated with plan_path and completion timestamp

## Files Modified
- cli/wizard.ts (extend with output generation)
- cli/commands/new.ts (implement --push-artifacts)

title: T6.4: flywheel new command (idea → wizard pipeline → output)
description:
## What
Implement the flywheel new command handler that wires together config loading,
wizard pipeline, output generation, and the gate machine.

## Command Signature
```
flywheel new <idea> [--models <m1,m2,m3>] [--fast] [--push-artifacts] [--skip-model <model>]
```

## Flow
1. Load configs (ssh, providers, prompts)
2. Initialize DB, create wizard_runs entry
3. Resolve plan model slots from providers.yaml (or --models override)
4. Run wizard pipeline (T6.2)
5. Generate outputs (T6.3)
6. Log total cost
7. Print summary:
   ```
   ✓ Plan generated for "Build a habit tracker"
     Models: claude-opus-4-6, gpt-4o, gemini-1.5-pro
     API calls: 8 | Tokens: 45,200 in / 28,100 out
     Cost: $2.34
     Plan: ~/.flywheel/runs/1/plan.md
     Log:  ~/.flywheel/runs/1/wizard-log.jsonl
   ```
8. If --push-artifacts, SCP to VPS
9. Set gate to GATE_PLAN (wizard complete, awaiting plan approval)

## --skip-model
If a model failed in a previous run, user can skip it:
flywheel new "idea" --skip-model gpt-4o
This removes GPT-4o from the fan-out and proceeds with 2 models.
