title: EPIC: T1 — SSH Layer (Foundation)
description:
## Overview
The SSH layer is the absolute foundation of the entire console. Every single
remote operation — NTM commands, br queries, VPS health checks, log streaming —
flows through this layer. Nothing else works without it.

## Architecture Decision: Single Persistent Connection
We use node-ssh (wrapper around ssh2) with a single persistent connection per
session. This is deliberate:
- The console targets ONE VPS (ubuntu@217.216.93.175)
- Multiple connections would waste file descriptors and complicate state
- Keepalive (10s interval, 3 max) prevents silent drops
- NO auto-reconnect — fail loudly, let the user decide

## Critical PATH Requirement
SSH non-interactive sessions strip PATH on the VPS. Every remote exec MUST use:
  bash -lc 'command here'
This is non-negotiable. Without it, ntm, br, bv, etc. are invisible.

## Files
- cli/ssh.ts (main implementation)
- cli/config.ts (SSH config loading — shared with T2)

## Acceptance Criteria
- flywheel ssh test prints latency and exits 0
- Failed connect exits 1 with clear error message
- All remote exec uses bash -lc wrapper
- Connection timing logged to ssh_connections table
- No retry/reconnect logic (rabbit hole guard)

## Rabbit Holes to Avoid
- SSH reconnection logic — fail loudly only
- Connection pooling — single connection is sufficient
- Agent forwarding / tunnel setup — out of scope

title: T1.1: SSH config loader (ssh.yaml → Zod-validated SshConfig)
T1.2: SSHManager class (persistent connection with keepalive)
T1.3: flywheel settings ssh (interactive config wizard)
description:
## What
Implement the flywheel settings ssh command — an interactive wizard that creates
or updates ~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml with validated SSH connection parameters.

## Why
First-run experience. Without ssh.yaml, nothing works. The wizard should guide
the user through setup and validate the connection before saving.

## Implementation Details

### File: cli/commands/settings.ts (new)

Uses Node.js readline (no extra dependency).

### Flow:
1. Check for existing ~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml, load as defaults if present
2. Prompt for: host, user (default: ubuntu), port (default: 22), key_path, remote_repo_root
3. Validate key_path exists locally (fs.existsSync)
4. Write to ~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml
5. Attempt SSH connection (calls sshManager.connect())
6. On success: print "✓ Connected to user@host — Xms"
7. On failure: print error, ask if user wants to re-enter values

### mkdir -p
Create ~/.flywheel/ if it doesn't exist.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Creates ~/.flywheel/ directory if missing
- Loads existing ssh.yaml as defaults for re-configuration
- Validates key file exists before attempting connection
- Tests connection after save
- Clear error on connection failure with option to retry

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/settings.ts (new)
- cli/index.ts (wire up command)

title: T1.4: flywheel ssh test (connection validation + tool discovery)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel ssh test — connects to VPS, measures latency, checks that
all required tools are accessible, prints results, logs to ssh_connections table.

## Why
This is the primary debugging and validation command. It answers: "Can I reach
my VPS and are all the flywheel tools available?"

## Implementation Details

### File: cli/commands/ssh.ts (new)

### Flow:
1. Load SSH config via loadSshConfig()
2. Connect via sshManager.connect() — capture latency
3. Run tool checks in parallel (Promise.all):
   - ntm --robot-version → extract version
   - br --version → extract version
   - which bv → check exists
   - which ubs → check exists
   - which dcg → check exists
   - which cass → check exists
   - git --version → extract version
   - gh --version → extract version
4. Print results:
   ✓ Connected to ubuntu@217.216.93.175 (port 22) — 24ms
   ✓ NTM v1.2.0 available
   ✓ br v0.x available
   ✓ bv available
   ⚠ ubs not found (optional)
   ✓ dcg available
   ✓ cass available
5. Log to ssh_connections table (requires T2 db to be available)
6. Exit code: 0 if all required tools found, 1 if connection fails, 2 if optional tools missing

### Required vs Optional Tools
Required (hard fail): ntm, br, git, gh
Optional (warn only): bv, ubs, dcg, cass

### Notes
- Tool version extraction should be forgiving — parse what you can, show "available" if version unparseable
- Each tool check has its own 5s timeout
- If DB not initialized yet (first run), skip logging — don't fail

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prints latency for SSH connection
- Shows version for each tool when parseable
- Required tool missing → exit 1 with clear message
- Optional tool missing → warning, still exit 0
- Logs connection to ssh_connections table when DB available

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/ssh.ts (new)
- cli/index.ts (wire up command)

title: T1.5: Remote log streaming (SSH stream + LineBuffer)
description:
## What
Implement the SSHManager.stream() method and the streaming infrastructure used
by the monitor and dashboard to show live agent output from the VPS.

## Why
The dashboard (T11) and monitor (T9) need real-time output from agents. This
flows through SSH stream → LineBuffer → WebSocket. This bead covers the SSH
side; the WebSocket side is in T9.

## Implementation Details

Uses node-ssh exec() in raw stream mode. The stream() method:
1. Calls this.conn.exec(wrappedCmd) to get an SSH Channel
2. Pipes stdout/stderr through LineBuffer instances
3. Each line emitted to onLine callback with stream tag ('out' | 'err')
4. AbortSignal listener calls channel.close() to terminate
5. Returns a Promise that resolves when the channel closes

### Cancellation
AbortSignal is critical — the monitor must be able to cancel streams when
the user switches sessions or stops monitoring.

### Error Handling
- If SSH connection drops mid-stream, emit an error line and resolve the promise
- Don't throw — the caller (monitor) handles reconnection at a higher level

## Acceptance Criteria
- stream() emits complete lines (not partial chunks)
- AbortSignal cancels the stream cleanly
- Connection drop during stream doesn't throw unhandled rejection
- Works with ntm --robot-markdown and br list --json (both tested)

## Files Modified
- cli/ssh.ts (add stream method implementation + LineBuffer class)

title: EPIC: T4 — NTM Bridge (Typed Robot API Wrapper)
description:
## Overview
The NTM Bridge is the typed TypeScript wrapper around NTM robot API commands
executed over SSH. It translates between the console TypeScript world and the
NTM JSON CLI world.

## Why a Separate Bridge Layer
NTM output is... messy:
1. May emit log lines before/after JSON (need extractJson guard)
2. Pane ID format inconsistency: --robot-status uses %41, --robot-snapshot uses 0.1
3. Error messages vary by command
4. Some commands return arrays, others objects
5. br (beads) commands have a separate output format

The bridge normalizes ALL of this into clean TypeScript interfaces. Upper layers
(commands, wizard, dashboard) never see raw NTM output.

## Architecture
```
Commands / Wizard / Dashboard
        ↓
    NtmBridge (this layer)
        ↓
    SSHManager.exec()
        ↓
    bash -lc 'ntm --robot-*'  or  bash -lc 'br --json ...'
```

## Pane ID Normalization
NTM uses two formats:
- --robot-status: %41 (tmux internal ID)
- --robot-snapshot: 0.1 (window.pane index)
The bridge normalizes to 0.1 format everywhere. This is critical because
the dashboard and prompt engine reference agents by pane ID.

## Key Types (from verified VPS output)
- NtmAgent: pane, type, type_confidence, state, last_output_age_sec, current_bead, pending_mail
- NtmSession: name, attached, agents[]
- NtmSnapshot: ts, sessions[], beads_summary, agent_mail, alerts[]
- NtmSendResult: session, sent_at, targets[], successful[], failed[]
- NtmRecipe: name, description, source, total_agents, agents[]

## Files
- cli/ntm-bridge.ts (main implementation)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 7 robot API commands wrapped with typed return values
- JSON extraction guard handles log noise before/after JSON
- Pane IDs normalized to window.pane format everywhere
- br list/ready/blocked/stats/show --json wrapped for bead queries
- All API calls logged to api_calls table (free for NTM, but structure holds)
- beadsList() returns [] when .beads/ not initialized on VPS

title: T4.1: JSON extraction guard (extractJson utility)
T4.2: NtmBridge core methods (snapshot, status, markdown, recipes, version)
description:
## What
Implement the read-only NTM robot API methods in the NtmBridge class.

## Methods

### snapshot(): Promise<NtmSnapshot>
- Calls: ntm --robot-snapshot
- Returns: Full per-agent state with type_confidence, last_output_age_sec, current_bead, pending_mail
- This is the PRIMARY polling method used by dashboard and monitor

### status(): Promise<NtmStatus>
- Calls: ntm --robot-status
- Returns: Full tmux session state (more detail than snapshot)
- Note: Uses %41 pane IDs — must normalize to 0.1 format

### markdown(): Promise<string>
- Calls: ntm --robot-markdown
- Returns: Human-readable status table (raw markdown string)
- Used by flywheel monitor for terminal display

### recipes(): Promise<NtmRecipe[]>
- Calls: ntm --robot-recipes
- Returns: 6 named presets (quick-claude, full-stack, minimal, etc.)
- Used by swarm command to validate recipe names

### version(): Promise<NtmVersion>
- Calls: ntm --robot-version
- Returns: Version metadata
- Used by ssh test and preflight

### sessions(): Promise<NtmSessionSummary[]>
- Calls: ntm --robot-sessions
- Returns: Fast minimal session list (name + attached status)
- Lighter than full snapshot for quick checks

## Pane ID Normalization
All methods that return pane IDs must normalize from %41 to 0.1 format.
Implement normalizePaneId() helper that maps between formats.

## Type Definitions
Define all interfaces (NtmSnapshot, NtmAgent, NtmSession, NtmRecipe, etc.)
in the same file. These were verified against live VPS output on 2026-03-09.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 6 methods return typed objects
- Pane IDs consistently in 0.1 format
- All calls logged to api_calls table
- 30s default timeout per call
- NtmParseError on invalid JSON with helpful message

title: T4.3: NtmBridge mutation methods (spawn, send, pause, resume)
description:
## What
Implement the write/mutation NTM robot API methods in NtmBridge.

## Methods

### spawn(session: string, recipe: string, opts?): Promise<void>
- Calls: ntm --robot-spawn (or ntm spawn with recipe)
- Spawns a new tmux session with agents per the recipe
- opts: { cc?: number, cod?: number, gmi?: number } — model count overrides
- Note: NTM spawn is slow (10-30s) — use longer timeout

### send(session: string, msg: string, target: SendTarget): Promise<NtmSendResult>
- Calls: ntm --robot-send <session> --msg="<msg>"
- Target types: { all?: boolean, pane?: string, type?: string }
- Returns: sent_at, targets[], successful[], failed[]
- This is the CORE method for prompt injection

### pause(session: string): Promise<void>
- Pauses all agents in a session
- Used by budget guard and manual intervention

### resume(session: string): Promise<void>
- Resumes paused agents
- Used after budget review or manual intervention

## SendTarget Resolution
```typescript
type SendTarget = 
  | { all: true }                    // broadcast to all agents
  | { pane: string }                 // specific agent by pane ID
  | { type: 'claude' | 'codex' | 'gemini' }  // all agents of a type
```
For type-based targeting, get snapshot first, filter agents, send to each.

## Error Handling
- spawn failure: throw with recipe name and NTM error
- send partial failure: return NtmSendResult with failed[] array (don't throw)
- pause/resume: throw on failure (these are critical operations)

## Acceptance Criteria
- spawn() creates session with correct agent count
- send() returns structured result with success/failure per agent
- pause()/resume() work on active sessions
- All mutations logged to api_calls and phase_events tables

## Files Modified
- cli/ntm-bridge.ts (add mutation methods to NtmBridge class)

title: T4.4: NtmBridge bead methods (beadsList, beadsStats, beadShow)
EPIC: T7 — Prompt Engine (Variable Resolution + Send Pipeline)
description:
## Overview
The Prompt Engine is the layer between the prompt library (YAML) and NTM
agent injection. It resolves {variables}, routes to the correct model, and
maintains an audit trail of every prompt sent.

## Why a Separate Engine
The prompts send command (T5.3) handles the CLI interface, but the engine
is also used by:
- The wizard (T6) for plan/synthesis prompts
- The swarm (T8) for init-briefing and bead-claim prompts
- The dashboard (T11) for the PromptLibrary panel
- The review (T10) for fresh-review and peer-review prompts

Centralizing this logic ensures consistent variable resolution and logging
regardless of the caller.

## Variable Resolution Priority
1. Explicit vars parameter (from command args or API call)
2. Current flywheel run context (from flywheel_runs table)
3. VPS environment (remote_repo_root from ssh.yaml)
4. Unknown variables: leave as-is with warning log

## Files
- cli/prompt-engine.ts (main implementation)

## Acceptance Criteria
- resolve() substitutes all known {variables}
- send() calls NtmBridge and logs to prompt_sends table
- list() returns all prompts with optional phase filter
- Unknown variables logged as warnings (not errors)

title: T7.1: PromptEngine class (resolve + send + list)
T7.2: Run context variable resolution (SQLite → prompt vars)
description:
## What
Implement getRunContextVar() — resolves {variable} names from the current
flywheel run state stored in SQLite.

## Variable Mapping
- {project_name} → flywheel_runs.project_name
- {plan_path} → wizard_runs.plan_path (most recent for this project)
- {plan_summary} → first 500 chars of plan.md content (read from disk)
- {checkpoint_sha} → flywheel_runs.checkpoint_sha
- {session_name} → derived from project_name + run_id
- {bead_assignments} → serialized list of ready beads from last snapshot
- {commit_rules} → hardcoded commit agent safety rules

## Why Hardcoded commit_rules
The commit agent safety constraints are NON-CONFIGURABLE by design:
- May only git add already-tracked files
- May only git commit and git push
- Must never: rebase, merge, reset --hard, checkout ., deploy, git add untracked
These are injected via {commit_rules} variable in swarm-init-briefing prompt.

## Files Modified
- cli/prompt-engine.ts (implement getRunContextVar)

title: EPIC: T5 — Core CLI Commands (settings, ssh, preflight, prompts)
description:
## Overview
The first user-facing commands that tie together the SSH, config, and NTM bridge
layers into working CLI tools. These are the commands a user runs during initial
setup and ongoing operation.

## Commands in This Epic
1. flywheel settings ssh — interactive SSH config wizard
2. flywheel ssh test — connection validation + tool discovery
3. flywheel preflight [--force] — full VPS readiness check
4. flywheel prompts list — browse prompt library
5. flywheel prompts send — inject prompts to agents
6. flywheel init <name> — create project on VPS

## Why These Commands First
These are the "plumbing" commands. You cannot use the wizard (T6), swarm (T8),
or dashboard (T11) without first: (a) configuring SSH, (b) verifying VPS tools,
(c) having a prompt library. These commands also serve as integration tests for
T1-T4.

## Command File Organization
Each command group gets its own file in cli/commands/:
- cli/commands/settings.ts
- cli/commands/ssh.ts
- cli/commands/preflight.ts
- cli/commands/prompts.ts
- cli/commands/init.ts

Each file exports a function that registers subcommands on the commander program.

## Acceptance Criteria
- flywheel settings ssh → interactive config, validates connection
- flywheel ssh test → latency + tool versions
- flywheel preflight → exit codes 0/2/3 as specified
- flywheel prompts list → ≥25 prompts grouped by phase
- flywheel prompts send <name> --all → sends to agents via NTM
- flywheel init <name> → creates project directory on VPS

title: T5.1: flywheel preflight (VPS readiness check with exit codes)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel preflight [--force] — a comprehensive VPS readiness checker
that validates all required and optional tools are available.

## Exit Code Contract (non-negotiable)
- 0: All checks pass (required + optional)
- 2: Required pass, optional warnings — allowed with --force
- 3: Required check failed — must fix before proceeding

## Required Tools (exit 3 if missing)
- ntm (agent orchestrator)
- br (beads issue tracker)
- git (version control)
- gh (GitHub CLI — for deploy phase)

## Optional Tools (exit 2 if missing, warn)
- bv (beads TUI viewer)
- ubs (universal build system)
- dcg (dead code guardian)
- cass (context-aware session summarizer)

## Implementation
```typescript
interface CheckResult {
  tool: string;
  found: boolean;
  version?: string;
  required: boolean;
}
```

Run all checks in parallel via Promise.all, then format output:
```
✓ ntm v1.2.0 — required
✓ br v0.x — required
✓ git v2.43.0 — required
✓ gh v2.40.0 — required
✓ bv v0.x — optional
⚠ ubs — not found (optional)
✓ dcg v0.x — optional
⚠ cass — not found (optional)

Result: 2 optional tools missing. Use --force to proceed.
```

## Exit Code Logic
```typescript
const requiredMissing = results.filter(r => r.required && !r.found);
const optionalMissing = results.filter(r => !r.required && !r.found);
if (requiredMissing.length > 0) process.exit(3);
if (optionalMissing.length > 0 && !opts.force) process.exit(2);
process.exit(0);
```

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/preflight.ts (new)
- cli/index.ts (wire up)

title: T5.3: flywheel prompts send (inject prompt to agent via NTM)
description:
## What
Implement flywheel prompts send <name> [--agent <pane>] [--all] [--type <type>]
— looks up a prompt in the library, resolves variables, and sends to agent(s)
via NTM robot-send.

## Why
This is how the human operator communicates with agents. Every prompt injection
flows through this command (or its dashboard equivalent). It provides:
- Named, versioned prompts (not ad-hoc strings)
- Variable substitution from run context
- Audit trail (logged to prompt_sends table)
- Model routing metadata (consumed by NTM)

## Implementation

### Variable Resolution
1. Load prompt text from prompts.yaml
2. Get current run context from SQLite (flywheel_runs)
3. Replace {variable} patterns:
   - {project_name} → from current run
   - {plan_path} → from wizard_runs
   - {bead_id}, {bead_title} → from command args or current assignment
   - Unknown vars → leave as-is with warning

### Send Flow
1. Resolve prompt text with variables
2. Determine target: --all → broadcast, --agent <pane> → specific, --type <type> → filtered
3. Call NtmBridge.send(session, resolvedText, target)
4. Log to prompt_sends table: (prompt_name, agent_target, sent_at, run_id)
5. Print result: "Sent 'fresh-review' to 4 agents (3 successful, 1 failed)"

### Session Discovery
If no explicit session, use the session from current flywheel_run.
If no current run, use the most recent NTM session from robot-sessions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Looks up prompt by name, fails with suggestion if not found
- Resolves {variables} from run context + explicit args
- Sends via NTM bridge and shows success/failure counts
- Logs every send to prompt_sends table
- --all broadcasts to all agents in session
- --agent targets specific pane
- --type targets all agents of a given type

## Files Modified
- cli/commands/prompts.ts (extend with send subcommand)

title: T5.5: loadPrompts() config loader (prompts.yaml → typed Prompt map)
description:
## What
Implement loadPrompts() in cli/config.ts — loads config/prompts.yaml and
validates each prompt has the required fields (text, model, effort, phase).

## Zod Schema
```typescript
const PromptSchema = z.object({
  text: z.string().min(1),
  model: z.enum(['opus', 'gpt-4o', 'gemini', 'any']),
  effort: z.enum(['max', 'high', 'medium', 'low']),
  phase: z.enum(['plan', 'beads', 'swarm', 'review', 'deploy']),
});

const PromptsFileSchema = z.object({
  prompts: z.record(z.string(), PromptSchema),
});
```

## Loading Strategy
- Look for prompts.yaml in: (1) project config/ dir, (2) ~/.flywheel/prompts.yaml
- Project-local takes precedence (allows per-project prompt overrides)
- Validate ALL prompts at once, show all errors (safeParse)

T5.6: flywheel doctor (environment health check)
description:
## What
Implement 'flywheel doctor' command that validates the entire local + remote environment.

## Checks (ordered)
1. Node >= 20 (local)
2. npm dependencies installed (local)
3. SQLite DB accessible and schema current (local)
4. providers.yaml exists and parses (local)
5. ssh.yaml exists and parses (local)
6. SSH connection to VPS works (remote)
7. ntm --robot-version responds (remote)
8. br --version responds (remote)
9. tmux installed (remote)
10. Git repo accessible at remote_repo_root (remote)

## Output Format
Each check prints: ✓ check-name ... ok | ✗ check-name ... FAIL: reason
Exit code 0 if all pass, 1 if any fail.

## Why
First command a new user should run. Catches misconfiguration before
it manifests as cryptic errors deep in the pipeline.

- cli/commands/doctor.ts (new command)
- cli/index.ts (register command)

title: T5.8: flywheel runs list/show (inspect flywheel run history)
description:
## What
Implement 'flywheel runs list' and 'flywheel runs show <id>' commands.

### runs list
- Queries flywheel_runs table, shows: ID, project, current_phase, created_at, status
- Default: last 20 runs, --all for unlimited
- Color-coded by status: green=DONE, yellow=active phase, red=FAILED

### runs show <id>
- Detailed view of a single run: all columns from flywheel_runs
- Plus: phase_events for that run (timeline)
- Plus: wizard_runs linked to it
- Plus: total API cost from api_calls table

## Why
Post-mortem inspection of past flywheel runs. Essential for understanding
what happened, how much it cost, and whether the pipeline worked.
